title: Hand-carved beech bowl
subtitle: A shallow serving bowl carved from a seasoned beech blank using gouges and inshave
excerpt: Beech is the traditional British bowl wood: close-grained, hard enough to last in kitchen use, and light enough in colour not to stain. A shallow serving bowl carved from a prepared seasoned blank with gouges and an inshave is a manageable first bowl project; the blank is pre-dimensioned, the walls are not too thin, and beech holds an edge cleanly without tearing. This intermediate project covers roughing the outside with a gouge, hollowing the interior, and sanding to a food-safe linseed oil finish.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for gouge carving and bowl-making technique. Encyclopaedia Britannica 11th edition (1911) for beech as the traditional European bowl wood.

glossaryTerms.definition: Timber dried to a stable moisture content. A seasoned bowl blank will not distort after carving and is ready for immediate finishing.
The degree of curvature on a gouge blade. A low sweep number (3 to 5) is nearly flat; a high sweep number (9 to 11) is deeply curved. The sweep determines how deep a cut the gouge takes.
A heavily curved drawknife used with both hands to hollow the interior of bowls. Pulled toward the body in long strokes across the grain.

content.text: Beech has been the standard British bowl wood for centuries because it holds an edge cleanly, is hard enough for daily kitchen use, and is available from managed woodland. A
seasoned
blank cuts harder than green wood but produces a finished bowl that will not move after completion. The
gouge sweeps

used here are a 7 mm radius for the outside shaping and a 9 mm radius for the inside rough-out; both are driven by mallet rather than hand pressure because seasoned beech requires more force than softwood or green timber.
What you need: wood

A beech blank, 30 cm long, 20 cm wide, 7 cm thick, fully seasoned (available from specialist suppliers as a pre-dimensioned bowl blank). The blank should be free of knots and surface checks. Sycamore, cherry, or lime are all suitable alternatives; they carve slightly more easily than beech.
What you need: tools
A sweep-7 and sweep-9 gouge with a carpenters mallet. An
inshave
for the interior. An India oilstone and leather strop for maintaining the edge throughout. Sandpaper in 120, 180, and 240 grit. Raw linseed oil.
Safety
Secure the blank in a vice or against a bench stop before all mallet-driven gouge work. The gouge is driven away from the body; keep the free hand behind the cutting edge at all times.
The work
Step 1: Mark and rough the outside

Mark the bowl outline on the top face with a pencil. Draw the bowl plan (oval, 25 cm by 15 cm) and the target wall profile on the side of the blank: the base is flat, the walls rise at about 15 degrees outward, and the rim is 8 mm thick. Clamp the blank with the bottom face down. Use the sweep-7 gouge driven by mallet to remove the corners of the blank outside the marked oval, working across the grain from the high corners toward the rim. Score relief cuts every 5 cm before the main passes to prevent splits running beyond the intended cut.
Step 2: Hollow the interior

Re-clamp the blank with the top face up. Use the sweep-9 gouge to remove material from the bowl interior, working from the centre outward in overlapping passes. Keep 12 mm wall thickness throughout this stage. Switch to the inshave to remove gouge ridges from the interior surface; pull across the grain in long strokes. Strop both the gouge and the inshave every ten minutes; seasoned beech blunts edges faster than green wood.
Step 3: Thin the walls to final thickness

With the sweep-7 gouge, refine the outside profile to the target wall thickness of 8 mm at the rim tapering to 12 mm at the base. Check the thickness by measuring with fingers inside and outside simultaneously; any hot spots (noticeably thicker areas) can be felt as a ridge. Refine the inside with the inshave or a travisher to a smooth, consistent curve.
Step 4: Sand and finish

Sand the outside with 120 grit, then 180, then 240. Sand the inside with 120 and 180 (240 on the inside is unnecessary and over-polished wood absorbs oil less well). Apply raw linseed oil: three thin coats, one per day, wiping off excess after 30 minutes each time. Raw linseed oil is food-safe once fully set (allow 3 to 7 days after the final coat before food contact).
Variations
A deeper bowl for fruit or bread needs a blank 10 to 12 cm thick; the hollowing requires more passes but the technique is identical.
A sycamore bowl is slightly easier to carve than beech and has the same food-safe properties. Cherry gives a warm pink-brown colour that deepens over the first year of use.
Care
Hand-wash only. Re-oil when the surface looks dry or the colour dulls. Do not soak in water; prolonged saturation may cause the wood to check.

items.symptom: The gouge tears the surface rather than cutting clean chips
items.cause: Cutting against the grain, or the edge needs honing.
items.fix: Reverse the working direction and strop the gouge. On the curved inside of a bowl, the grain direction reverses around the curve; work from the centre outward on both sides.

items.symptom: The wall is uneven in thickness
items.cause: The gouge passes were not uniform in depth.
items.fix: Feel both inside and outside simultaneously with fingertips to locate thick areas. Remove material from the thicker areas with a light gouge pass rather than trying to even the whole surface.
